accepted.question_text: Let G be a graph. If P is a graph property and M is a matching of G whose induced subgraph <M> has property P, call M a P-matching. Write beta_P(G) for the maximum size of a P-matching in G and beta_P^-(G) for the minimum size of a maximal P-matching in G. For each P-matching family introduced in the survey, what nontrivial upper bounds can be proved for beta_P(G), and what nontrivial lower bounds can be proved for beta_P^-(G)?

evidence.quote: Definition 3.1. Let P be a property of G, and let M a matching of G such that the induced subgraph of M, <M>, has property P, then we call M a P-matching. With regard to P-matchings, we are interested in two parameters: beta_P = max{|M| : <M> has property P} beta_P^- = min{|M| : <M> is maximal with respect to P}.
Of course the first direction would be to find good upper bounds on beta_P and good lower bounds on beta_P^- for each of the P-matchings introduced.

accepted.question_text: Let G be a graph, let P be a graph property, and call a matching M of G a P-matching when the induced subgraph <M> has property P. For each P-matching family introduced in the survey, develop characterization theorems that determine exactly when a given matching M is a maximum P-matching, rather than merely a maximal one.

evidence.quote: Definition 3.1. Let P be a property of G, and let M a matching of G such that the induced subgraph of M, <M>, has property P, then we call M a P-matching.
Also for each P-matching, characterization theorems should be developed to describe when a given matching M is a maximum P-matching.

accepted.question_text: For a graph G and a graph property P, let beta_P(G) denote the maximum size of a matching M whose induced subgraph <M> has property P. Is there a graph parameter alpha_P, analogous to the Gallai identities for ordinary matchings, such that beta_P(G) + alpha_P(G) = |V(G)| for every graph G in the relevant class?

evidence.quote: Theorem 2.4 (Gallai Identities (1959)). If G has no isolates, then i) alpha_0(G)+beta_0(G) = |V(G)| ii) alpha_1(G)+beta_1(G) = |V(G)|.
With regard to P-matchings, we are interested in two parameters: beta_P = max{|M| : <M> has property P} beta_P^- = min{|M| : <M> is maximal with respect to P}.
Recalling the Gallai identities, it would be of interest to find a parameter, say "alpha_P" such that beta_P(G)+alpha_P(G) = |V(G)|.

accepted.question_text: For a graph G and a graph property P, let beta_P(G) denote the maximum size of a P-matching in G, where a P-matching is a matching M whose induced subgraph <M> has property P. What Nordhaus-Gaddum type bounds can be proved for beta_P over a graph and its complement; specifically, what are the best possible lower and upper bounds for beta_P(G) + beta_P(G_bar) and for beta_P(G) * beta_P(G_bar)?

evidence.quote: Definition 3.1. Let P be a property of G, and let M a matching of G such that the induced subgraph of M, <M>, has property P, then we call M a P-matching.
It would also be desirable to have Nordhaus-Gaddum type results for these parameters, i.e., to complete the following inequalities ? <= beta_P(G)+beta_P(G_bar) <=? ? <= beta_P(G) * beta_P(G_bar) <=?

accepted.question_text: For a graph G and a graph property P, let beta_P(G) be the maximum size of a matching M whose induced subgraph <M> has property P. For the P-matching parameters introduced in the survey, what is the computational complexity of determining beta_P(G), and for which graph classes can beta_P(G) be computed in polynomial time or shown NP-complete?

evidence.quote: With regard to P-matchings, we are interested in two parameters: beta_P = max{|M| : <M> has property P} beta_P^- = min{|M| : <M> is maximal with respect to P}.
In addition to many other results which could be of interest concerning these parameters, determining the complexity of finding beta_P is most desirable.

accepted.question_text: A disconnected matching is a matching M such that |M| = 1 or the induced subgraph <M> is disconnected, and beta_dc(G) denotes the maximum size of a disconnected matching in G. What is the computational complexity of determining beta_dc(G), especially since the survey notes complexity results for several other matching varieties but leaves beta_dc unresolved?

evidence.quote: Example 3.5 (Disconnected matching). A matching M is disconnected if |M| = 1 or <M> is disconnected. We have beta_dc(G) = max{|M| : M is disconnected matching} and beta_dc^-(G) = min{|M| : M is maximal disconnected matching}.
Of the matching varieties discussed in Section 3, this leaves only the complexity of beta_dc in question.

trace.notes: Mapped 8 pages. Sections identified: 1. Introduction; 2. Historical Background; 3. Varieties of Matchings; 4. New Classes of Matchings; 5. Complexity Results; 6. Open Questions. Definitions and notation for P-matchings appear on pages 3-7, including Definition 3.1 and Definition 4.1-4.8. Theorem and proposition labels found: Theorem 2.1-2.4 on page 2, Proposition 3.1 and Theorem 3.2-3.3 on page 4. Likely open-problem regions are page 7 (implicit complexity question for beta_dc) and pages 7-8 (Section 6 open questions).

Rewrote the Nordhaus-Gaddum prompt into a standalone question by defining beta_P and spelling out the sum and product bounds over G and its complement.
Expanded the final open direction into a self-contained complexity question for beta_P over the introduced P-matching families.
Reconstructed the unresolved complexity question for disconnected matchings by inlining the definition of a disconnected matching and beta_dc.
